inputs.story: Help Jackie research her article on the oldest living people in America by matching each centenarian to their age, hometown and state. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.
2. Kyle, the centenarian who is 109 years old and the person who is 110 years old are all different people.
3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
5. The person who is 111 years old is a native of South Dakota.
inputs.constants:
names: Arlene; Ernesto; Kyle; Willard.
ages: 109 years; 110 years; 111 years; 112 years.
states: Kansas; Louisiana; Pennsylvania; South Dakota.
inputs.solution:
names	ages	states
ernesto	110 years	kansas 
willard	109 years	louisiana 
kyle	112 years	pennsylvania 
arlene	111 years	south dakota

constants_formatting.prompt:
Given categorized constants of the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n", format the category and constants such that:
each category consists of only lowercase letters and underscores, and
each constant is either an integer or a string surrounded by double quotes, e.g., "United States".

There are two ways below to format constants and we must use the same way for all constants of the same category.
1. Turn all constants of the same category into integers with no space or special character.
2. Add double quotes around all constants of the same category.
Note that the 1st way has a higher priority, meaning that we must turn all constants of the same category into integers whenever possible. For example, twice or second can be turned into 2, September can be turned into 9, September 5th can be turned into 5 if all dates are in September, but 9:30am can only be turned into "9:30am" since no integer can represent 9:30am.

Original constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood typse: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

Formatted constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Original constants:
months: January; April; October; December.
times: 8:30AM; 10:30AM; 2:30PM; 3:30PM.
durations: 1 day; 3 days; 11 days; 12 days.

Formatted constants:
months: 1; 4; 10; 12.
time: "8:30AM"; "10:30PM"; "2:30PM"; "3:30PM".
duration: 1; 3; 11; 12.

Original constants:
names: Arlene; Ernesto; Kyle; Willard.
ages: 109 years; 110 years; 111 years; 112 years.
states: Kansas; Louisiana; Pennsylvania; South Dakota.

predicates.prompt:
Given a problem and some categorized constants of the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n", generate the minimum number of predicates to define the relations among the categories of constants. Each generated predicate is of the form "predicate(X1, X2, ..., Xn)" where X1, X2, ..., Xn are different variables and each variable X belongs to one of the categories. For each category, there must exist at least one variable of some predicate that belongs to this category.

Problem 1:
Consider N-Queens Puzzle on a chessboard of size 8x8. The goal is to assign 8 queens on the chessboard so that no two queens can share the same row, column, or diagonal.

Constants:
index_of_row: 1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8.
index_of_column: 1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8.

Predicates:
% The categories in Constants include index_of_row and index_of_column. We use different variables Ir and Ic to represent index_of_row and index_of_column.
% We assign a queen at row Ir and column Ic, where Ir belongs to index_of_row and Ic belongs to index_of_column.
assign(Ir, Ic)

Problem 2:
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories in Constants include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

Problem 3:
Help Jackie research her article on the oldest living people in America by matching each centenarian to their age, hometown and state. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.
2. Kyle, the centenarian who is 109 years old and the person who is 110 years old are all different people.
3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
5. The person who is 111 years old is a native of South Dakota.

Constants:
names: "Arlene"; "Ernesto"; "Kyle"; "Willard"
ages: 109; 110; 111; 112
states: "Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota"

Predicates:
predicates.thinking:
Okay, let's tackle Problem 3. The goal is to generate the minimum number of predicates that define the relations among the categories of constants. The constants given are names, ages, and states. Each predicate should connect variables from these categories.

First, I need to understand what the problem requires. The user mentioned that each predicate should have variables from different categories, and each category must have at least one variable in some predicate. The constants are:

- names: "Arlene", "Ernesto", "Kyle", "Willard"
- ages: 109, 110, 111, 112
- states: "Kansas", "Louisiana", "Pennsylvania", "South Dakota"

From the previous examples, like Problem 2, they used a single predicate "match(E, P, W)" that connects all three categories. But maybe for Problem 3, since there are three categories (names, ages, states), the predicate should be something like "centenarian(Name, Age, State)".

Wait, but the problem says "minimum number of predicates". So maybe one predicate is enough. Let me check the problem statement again. It says "each variable X belongs to one of the categories", and "each category must have at least one variable in some predicate".

So, if I have a predicate like "centenarian(Name, Age, State)", then Name is from names, Age from ages, State from states. That covers all three categories. Each category is represented by a variable in the predicate. So that's one predicate. But I need to confirm if that's sufficient.

Looking at the clues:

1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.
   - So Kyle's age = Arlene's age +1.

2. Kyle, the 109-year-old, and the 110-year-old are all different people.
   - So Kyle ≠ 109, Kyle ≠ 110.

3. Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
   - Pennsylvania state person's age > Ernesto's age.

4. Ernesto is older than Louisiana native.
   - Ernesto's age > Louisiana state person's age.

5. 111-year-old is from South Dakota.
   - Age 111 → State South Dakota.

So, all these clues relate the three categories. Therefore, a single predicate that links name, age, and state should be enough. The predicate would be something like "centenarian(Name, Age, State)".

Wait, but in Problem 2, they used "match(E, P, W)", which is a single predicate. So for Problem 3, similarly, the predicate should be "centenarian(Name, Age, State)".

But let's make sure. The problem says "generate the minimum number of predicates". Since all three categories need to be connected, and a single predicate can cover all three, that's the minimum (one predicate). If we tried to split into more predicates, like one for name and age, another for age and state, etc., that would be more than one, but the minimum is one.

Wait, but the problem says "each variable X belongs to one of the categories". In the predicate "centenarian(Name, Age, State)", Name is from names, Age from ages, State from states. So each variable is from a different category. So that's correct.

Also, each category must have at least one variable in some predicate. Here, names is covered by Name, ages by Age, states by State. So all categories are covered.

Therefore, the minimum number is one predicate: "centenarian(Name, Age, State)".

Wait, but the problem says "the minimum number of predicates". So the answer should be that predicate.

But let me check if there's a reason to have more. For example, in some logic puzzles, sometimes you need to have additional predicates for inequalities or other relations. But the problem states that the predicates are to define the relations among the categories, and the examples given (like Problem 2) used a single predicate. The actual clues would be handled as constraints on that predicate, but the problem here is just to generate the predicate(s), not to write the actual constraints.

So the answer should be a single predicate that includes all three categories.

Wait, looking at Problem 1: the predicate is "assign(Ir, Ic)" where Ir is index_of_row and Ic is index_of_column. So two categories, one predicate.

Problem 2: three categories, one predicate "match(E, P, W)".

Problem 3: three categories (names, ages, states), so one predicate "centenarian(Name, Age, State)".

Yes, that makes sense. So the answer should be that predicate.
predicates.response:
To define the relations among the categories of constants for Problem 3, the minimum number of predicates required is **one**. This single predicate connects all three categories (names, ages, and states) into a unified relation, satisfying the requirement that each category is represented by at least one variable in the predicate.

---

### **Predicates:**
```prolog
centenarian(Name, Age, State)
```

---

### **Explanation:**
- **Categories covered:**
  - `Name` belongs to `names`: `"Arlene", "Ernesto", "Kyle", "Willard"`.
  - `Age` belongs to `ages`: `109, 110, 111, 112`.
  - `State` belongs to `states`: `"Kansas", "Louisiana", "Pennsylvania", "South Dakota"`.
- **Why one predicate is sufficient:**
  - All clues (1–5) relate the three categories (e.g., "Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene" involves `names` and `ages`; "Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto" involves `states` and `ages`).
  - A single predicate `centenarian(Name, Age, State)` allows all such relationships to be expressed as constraints on the same relation (e.g., `centenarian("Kyle", Age1, _)`, `centenarian("Arlene", Age2, _)`, with `Age1 = Age2 + 1`).
  - Using more than one predicate would be unnecessary and violate the "minimum number" requirement.

---

### **Conclusion:**
The minimal and sufficient predicate to define all relations among the categories is:

**`centenarian(Name, Age, State)`**

search_space.prompt:
Given some categorized constants in the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n" and some predicates about the relation among different categories of constants, write ASP (Answer Set Programming) rules to generate the search space of possible relations. In your response, include a SINGLE ASP code-block with the rules - it will be automatically parsed from your response.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

ASP Rules:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% For each employee E, it matches with exactly 1 price P and 1 wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).
```

Constants:
names: "Arlene"; "Ernesto"; "Kyle"; "Willard"
ages: 109; 110; 111; 112
states: "Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota"

Predicates:
centenarian(Name, Age, State)

ASP Rules:

ages(109; 110; 111; 112).

Then, the predicate is centenarian(Name, Age, State), which matches each name to an age and a state.

Similarly, here, for each name, exactly one age and one state. So the rule should be {centenarian(Name, Age, State): ages(Age), states(State)}=1 :- names(Name).

So the ASP rule should be:

{centenarian(Name, Age, State) : ages(Age), states(State)} = 1 :- names(Name).

Yes, that's the same structure as the example. The example used {match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E). So replacing match with centenarian, and the categories accordingly.

Let me check the constants again. The names are "Arlene", etc., ages are 109-112, states are the four listed. The predicate is centenarian, which probably means that each person (name) has a specific age (which is a centenarian, so age >=100, but the ages given are all 109+, so that's covered). The problem is to generate the search space of possible relations, so each name must be matched with exactly one age and one state.

So the ASP rules should define the categories as facts, then the rule that for each name, exactly one centenarian fact exists with that name, paired with some age and state.

So the code should look like:

names("Arlene"; "Ernesto"; "Kyle"; "Willard").
ages(109; 110; 111; 112).
states("Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota").

{centenarian(Name, Age, State) : ages(Age), states(State)} = 1 :- names(Name).

Wait, the example used "match(E, P, W)" as the predicate, so here it's "centenarian(Name, Age, State)".

Yes, so the rule is correct. Let me make sure that the categories are correctly defined. The constants are given as:

names: ... so the predicate is names/1.

Similarly for ages and states.

So the ASP code would have those three lines defining the constants, then the rule.

In the example, the constants were defined as employee("Bonita"; ...), so same here.

So the code should be:

ages(109; 110; 111; 112).
states("Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota").

{centenarian(Name, Age, State) : ages(Age), states(State)} = 1 :- names(Name).

That's the same structure as the example. The only difference is the predicate name and the category names.

I think that's it. Let me check if the user wants any other rules, but the problem says "generate the search space of possible relations", so the only rule needed is the one ensuring each name has exactly one age and state. The example used the same approach, so this should be correct.

```asp
names("Arlene"; "Ernesto"; "Kyle"; "Willard").
```

paraphrasing.prompt:
Copy a sequence of numbered sentences.

If a sentence is of the form "N. Of A and B, one is C and the other is D", replace it with 3 sentences below.
N.1 A and B are different.
N.2 A is C or D.
N.3 B is C or D.

For every sentence, if it is not of the form "N. Of ... and ...", simply copy it without replacement. An easy way to determine if a sentence is not of the above form is to check if its first word is not of.

In the following example, one sentence is of the above form.
Given:
1. The squad from Grenada ended with 2 silver medals.
2. Of the team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals, one finished with 2 gold medals and the other finished with 1 gold medal.
Copy:
1. The squad from Grenada ended with 2 silver medals.
2.1 The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
2.2 The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
2.3 The team that won 10 silver medals finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.

In the following example, no sentence is of the above form.
Given:
1. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
2. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
Copy:
1. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
2. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Given:
1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.

prompt.content: You are a semantic parser to turn clues in a problem into logical rules using only provided constants and predicates.

Consider the constraint in the following form
<C1>; <C2>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, <L2>, ..., <Ln>.
which says that if the conjunction "<L1> and <L2> and ... and <Ln>" is true, then the disjunction of comparisons "<C1> or <C2> or ... or <Cm>" must be true.

One can also add a restriction that "exactly k of <C1>, <C2>, ..., <Cm> is true" by using the following form
{<C1>; <C2>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, <L2>, ..., <Ln>.

Given a problem, extract all constraints from the clues in the problem using only the provided constants and predicates. In your response, include a SINGLE ASP code-block with the constraints - it will be automatically parsed from your response.
Ok. I will only write constraints under the provided forms.

Problem 1:
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

Constraints:

```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.
```

Problem 2:
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold, silver and bronze medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
5. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
6. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
8. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
country: "Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman".
silver_medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
gold_medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

Predicates:
% The categories include country, silver_medals, and gold_medals. We use different variables C, S, and G to represent country, silver_medals, and gold_medals.
% We assign a country C with silver medals S and gold medals G, where C belongs to country, S belongs to silver_medals, and G belongs to gold_medals.
assign(C, S, G)

Constraints:

```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 5. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 6. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 8. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.
```

Problem 3:
Help Jackie research her article on the oldest living people in America by matching each centenarian to their age, hometown and state. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.

2. Kyle, the centenarian who is 109 years old and the person who is 110 years old are all different people.  
3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.  
4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.  
5. The person who is 111 years old is a native of South Dakota.

Constants:
names: "Arlene"; "Ernesto"; "Kyle"; "Willard"
ages: 109; 110; 111; 112
states: "Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota"

Predicates:
centenarian(Name, Age, State)

{N1=N2; A1=A2; S1=S2}=0 :- centenarian(N1,A1,S1), centenarian(N2,A2,S2), (N1,A1,S1)!=(N2,A2,S2).

```asp
A1 = A2 + 1 :- centenarian("Kyle", A1, _), centenarian("Arlene", A2, _).

% 2. Kyle, the 109-year-old, and the 110-year-old are all different people.
{Age=109; Age=110}=0 :- centenarian("Kyle", Age, _).

% 3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
P > E :- centenarian(_, P, "Pennsylvania"), centenarian("Ernesto", E, _).

% 4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
E > L :- centenarian("Ernesto", E, _), centenarian(_, L, "Louisiana").

State = "South Dakota" :- centenarian(_, 111, State).
```

constraints.extracted:
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{N1=N2; A1=A2; S1=S2}=0 :- centenarian(N1,A1,S1), centenarian(N2,A2,S2), (N1,A1,S1)!=(N2,A2,S2).

% 1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.
A1 = A2 + 1 :- centenarian("Kyle", A1, _), centenarian("Arlene", A2, _).

% 2. Kyle, the 109-year-old, and the 110-year-old are all different people.
{Age=109; Age=110}=0 :- centenarian("Kyle", Age, _).

% 3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
P > E :- centenarian(_, P, "Pennsylvania"), centenarian("Ernesto", E, _).

% 4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
E > L :- centenarian("Ernesto", E, _), centenarian(_, L, "Louisiana").

initial_run.asp_program:
names("Arlene"; "Ernesto"; "Kyle"; "Willard").
ages(109; 110; 111; 112).
states("Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota").

{centenarian(Name, Age, State) : ages(Age), states(State)} = 1 :- names(Name).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{N1=N2; A1=A2; S1=S2}=0 :- centenarian(N1,A1,S1), centenarian(N2,A2,S2), (N1,A1,S1)!=(N2,A2,S2).

% 1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.
A1 = A2 + 1 :- centenarian("Kyle", A1, _), centenarian("Arlene", A2, _).

% 2. Kyle, the 109-year-old, and the 110-year-old are all different people.
{Age=109; Age=110}=0 :- centenarian("Kyle", Age, _).

% 3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
P > E :- centenarian(_, P, "Pennsylvania"), centenarian("Ernesto", E, _).

% 4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
E > L :- centenarian("Ernesto", E, _), centenarian(_, L, "Louisiana").

% 5. The person who is 111 years old is a native of South Dakota.
State = "South Dakota" :- centenarian(_, 111, State).

final.ground_truth:
names	ages	states
ernesto	110 years	kansas 
willard	109 years	louisiana 
kyle	112 years	pennsylvania 
arlene	111 years	south dakota
